Risk: LLM-generated code can contain subtle bugs or incorrect logic that is incorporated into research software without adequate review, potentially leading to erroneous results in published research.
Mitigations: Treat AI-generated code with the same review standards as human-written code. Ensure test coverage includes edge cases that LLMs commonly mishandle. Document where AI tools were used in development.
Ownership: Individual RSEs and researchers, research software teams, institutions providing AI coding tools.
Examples: The University of Oxford policy on Generative AI in research specifies "generating code" as substantive use of GenAI, and suggest human judgement is required in all substantive interactions. https://www.ox.ac.uk/research/support-researchers/research-practice/policy-generative-ai-research

Risk: Claude is better at coding than me. I pay my rent through my ability to code.
AI use will cause RSEs to lose important skills in research software practice. This might have catastrophic effects on the future of research software.
Mitigations: Ensure training and development continue to instill relevant software skills, and that such skills continue to be held in high regard by employers.

Risk: Generative AI uses lots of energy and water resources. Over-use will lead to environmental degradation and ecosystem collapse.
Mitigations: Lobby institutions, governments and industry to regulate the use of AI and properly assess environmental impacts.

Risk: RSEs do what they do because they love to code - not because they love to talk to a chatbot. Use of AI may lead to RSEs feeling less valuable and less able to do what they love.
Mitigations: Use communities of practice for GenAI in RSE to help with the transition to AI empowered coding.
